chunks.text: Names. Um, but feel free to, um, introduce yourself. We have 10 minutes with you, and after you have finished, uh, speaking to your submission, there might be questions from members. Thank you.

Yeah. Please get started when you're ready. Good morning, Madam Chair and members of the committee. My name is Hannah Clo, and I appear on behalf of the Free Speech Union alongside Douglas Brown, one of the members of our board. We have made a submission on the telecommunications and other matters, bill, because it has significant implications for freedom of expression in New Zealand.

For that reason, we oppose the bill. Before I turn to the bill itself. It's worth noting how broad the telecommunications regime is. A telecommunication covers almost any communication sent electronically, uh, from texts, messages, and emails to Fiverr and satellite data. In short, the regulatory regime touches nearly all forms of digital communication in New Zealand.

In the time available, I want to focus on two provisions of the bill. First, the bill is trying to apply New Zealand laws and rules to companies based overseas if they offer services to New Zealand. These laws include consumer protection codes and rules, but also require full interception capability, meaning systems must be designed and maintained to assist surveillance agencies to obtain access to private communications.

This includes the duty to decrypt communications if the entity supplied the encryption. Second, the bill introduces a new enforcement power, allowing the Secretary of the Ministry of Business Business Innovation and Employment MBIE to suspend, revoke, or restrict radio and spectrum licenses.

That means if an overseas company is unwilling or unable to comply with our laws in relation to the consumer codes or surveillance, their license can be revoked. If they don't have a New Zealand spectrum license because they're piggybacking on another, the secretary can then threaten to revoke that license unless access to the overseas provider is blocked.

So what does that mean for free speech? Well, the bill significantly expands the ability of New Zealand surveillance agencies, New Zealand Police, N-Z-I-S-G-C-S-B, to access private communications. New Zealanders will no longer be able to rely on overseas based networks or applications, offering greater protection for private communications.

Provided a warrant is obtained, New Zealand surveillance agencies will have access to communications across virtually all platforms and networks used by New Zealanders. This is dangerous because private encrypted communication is essential for journalists protecting, uh, their sources, whistleblowers, exposing wrongdoing, individuals organizing politically or speak anonymously, and members of vulnerable or marginalized communicating communities communicating with each other.

These forms of communication are fundamental to accountability, dissent, and checks on government power. Not only can they be accessed by surveillance agencies, but any back doors into encrypted communications will be abused by criminals. Not only is the door being opened for our surveillance agencies, but is also being opened for anyone else who can work their way in.

Now, that is just one free speech issue. Content moderation and platform governance requirements can easily be added to these codes and rules, which will be enforceable against all platforms and networks, which the Secretary of MBIE can enforce compliance with by suspending and revoking licenses. So what we're looking at is the creation of a huge censorship tool by this bill, which will be sitting there just ready to abuse.

And finally, we are a small country faced with expanded interception, decryption, and enforcement obligations. Overseas companies may decide it's just not worth it and withdraw, withdraw their services from New Zealand rather than comply. If that happens, the New Zealanders will lose access to networks and services that they use for communicating with people overseas.

Uh, access to diverse sources of information, research and part participating generally in global intellectual, cultural and political debate. This isn't hypothetical. This has happened and is happening overseas. Just looking at the bill in the context of the other acts, in the regime within the regime, the Telecommunications Act, the Telecommunications Intersection Capability and Security Act, and the Radio Communications Act, it is, if it is even technically possible to achieve what it aims to, it will have significant negative impacts on freedom

of expression in this country. Now, that is an overview of our submission. I will now hand it over to Douglas Brown, uh, from our board to give him the opportunity to speak and to take the lead in responding to any questions that the committee may have. Thank you, Hannah. So the real, uh, focus that we have, or real concern that we have with this, uh, comes through with the interception capability that's being requested here, especially in regards to, uh, overseas based service providers.

And that's really, uh, where this hammer's home is the fact that the overseas service providers are almost all, uh, these days offering what is called end-to-end encrypted communications. Governments around the world have been very keen to gain what they call a backdoor, uh, what is, uh, called in the, uh, interception Telecom, telecommunications, interception and Communications Act, or tsa, uh, an interception capability, uh, into end-to-end encrypted, uh, communications.

Uh, it is mathematically impossible, and that is because, uh, when you get a backdoor into something, it is no longer end-to-end encrypted. The providers do not hold keys between the devices. They cannot interrupt the flow of the communication if there is a way of interrupting that flow of communication, uh, that is given to a government.

And that is what this bill is even essentially asking those providers to do. Uh, it is not just something that the government has. It is something that any bad actor can discover and crack. And we know for a fact that, uh, governments are not, uh, the most advanced people in the room.

Uh, the bad actors, in fact, are far superior in their ability to find those back doors and to crack them and to use them. Uh, we like to think that, uh, our security services are in fact superior to the, uh, foreign bad actors. But that is in fact not the truth. And we have found, again and again that government databases themselves, uh, have been cracked, uh, notwithstanding what is supposed to be world-leading security.

Uh, the fact is that in regimes, even Western regimes, uh, where there has been dissent, where there has been, uh, um, attacks on freedom of expression by those governments, and we only have to look back five years to have our own government, uh, describe itself as being the sole source of truth on a matter.

Uh, it is the fact that things like that where dissent can be met with, uh, ostracism, uh, with even, uh, threats of criminal action, if it goes far enough that the need for end-to-end encryption, for the need for the ability to communicate free of others, uh, or the state spying on you is necessary.

We have had plenty of, uh, um, what was often called by Eric Crampton, Helen, love, joy, uh, think of the children. It's about crime. It's about safety. But never have we had data, never have we had actual, uh, information telling us what is necess, why it is necessary.

And finally, the worst part of all of this is the fact that nobody has been told about this. This is being, uh, crept through without announcement, without being told, without, uh, the providers being notified. Uh, it's not mentioned in parliament. It's not being mentioned in the media. It is simply being placed in there with this capacity. It may not be activated now, but it could be activated in the future.

And the government can go to apps like Signal, WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, apple Messages, and say, we have this ability. Provide us with this interception capacity. Uh, or we can block your servers. Uh, I'll leave it there. Okay, great. Thank you. We have just one minute. Um, so very quickly I would ask, um, uh, is in the powers of the secretary to revoke our restrict licenses for non-compliance.

So non-compliance is just not just non-compliance. It also describes what does that mean? So the bill talks about the nature and the extent of the non-compliance, and there is like list of things that the secretary will be required to take into consideration before they can exercise their power. Is there something that you think should be added in that list, Hannah,

Added to the list of things that the secretary should consider Before exercising out to revoke for non-compliant? Well, I, I don't think that's the key issue there, because having, it's, it's still a very big power that the secretary has. So even if they're required to consider certain elements, they still have the power to do so and justify it.

Yeah. But it's for non-compliance, right? So, so you're saying that that should not be there at all. That's what you're saying? Exactly. We are raising, we're raising our concern with that power and how significant it is and what effect it has. Yeah. And that's why I was asking if there was something you wanted to add to the list, the things that they should consider before exercising that power.

So no, I get what you have to say. Thank you so much for your response. Thank you. Thank you for your submission. Thanks for your contribution. Thank you very much. Thank You. Bye. Uh, next we have fund RNZ and the Kiwi rocket on Zoom again.

Hi. Hi, how are you? Jay, thank you so much for your submission. We have, uh, 10 minutes with you. Thank you. Thank you very much. So while I can see some good intentions with this bill, I cannot support it in its current form. And one of the reasons is that end-to-end encryption is very important for journalism for a start.

There are people who are putting their livelihoods on the line for stories. They're putting their safety on the line. In some cases, sometimes they can't even reveal their own identities. And if their security of their contact details is compromised, they could cost them a lot.

And we don't wanna make people afraid to come forward with information about stories that could benefit society and get justice from people and expose corruption. So I am very concerned about the breadth, the breadth of this bill and the power that the secretary of the Ministry of Innovation and Employment has over telecommunication companies and other countries.

Because that's a good place to companies overseas is a good place to route information to avoid compromising security and confidentiality. And I cannot understand why the decision making is left up to one individual and not put through the legal system in the courts.

And at least having a committee and why a secretary, what qualifies a secretary exactly to make a decision about telecommunication infrastructure will, it affects so many facets of our society. A lot of our lives are online now. People rely on the internet for just about everything.

They rely on the internet to communicate, to pass on confidential information, to do business, to have our footprint expanded to the rest of the world because we're such a small country and we are quite geographically isolated, that we need overseas companies to actually be prepared to do business in New Zealand and not be afraid to do business in New Zealand because of the rules that I think are not written in such a way that is friendly towards business and friendly towards journalism.

I think that the whole bill needs a rework. And a big concern of mine is actually what would happen if various telecommunication companies and social media platforms just decided that doing business in New Zealand was too much trouble. I note that the Free Speech Union talks about how small our country is and how there isn't a lot to be gained for them and doing business here.

The benefits for us and them coming here far outweighs the business. The benefits of them doing business here. They could just withdraw from New Zealand and it wouldn't hurt them much, but it would hurt New Zealand as an awful lot in my opinion. And we have to talk about the fact that we have main newstream New Zealand mainstream media in New Zealand, and we also have alternative media.

And I know there may be mixed feelings about alternative media, but alternative media is a great leveler in society. Not just for free speech, but for getting out information that might be ignored by the mainstream media initially. And whether or not to agree with what is being communicated.

Free speech is important. It's actually a human right as well. And so we need places where people can go to express their divergent views. And some of them may not be popular, but the wider range of views that are able to be expressed in society, the healthier the debate can be in society.

We cannot have a restriction in information by gatekeepers who have a lot of power in the mainstream media. I'm not knocking mainstream media, I think that does a fantastic job. But sometimes I've noticed that there's certain information that doesn't get through on the mainstream media.

It gets through on the alternative media. And those servers are not in New Zealand. Those companies are not in New Zealand, they're in other countries. And also it connects us to the rest of the world. We could communicate to the rest of the world. Now what happens if, and it's not the case with this government, but what happens if we did end up with a corrupt government someday, just suppose we did, and our only lifeline to communicate to other countries because a mass censorship was put down on the whole country,

including the mainstream media. Akin to North Korea is the alternative media. But the Secretary of the Ministry innovation and employment thought that they'd breached rules and decided to cut off either their radio spectrum or their digital spectrum. And as far as I can see, there's no redress for those companies to actually appeal or even be given full information on why they're being cut off with the infrastructure in New Zealand.

How would people tell the rest of the world what is wrong? Because the laws at the moment are pretty good. But what happens if we get a corrupt government who brings in all kinds of laws that restricts free speech to the level of North Korea? Even the way that people become censored is by the gradual erosion of free speech, even unpopular speech.

If you take away people's avenues, you are running the risk of actually taking away something that could potentially save people. A social media blackout or an internet blackout is an absolute disaster, in my opinion. And also add to that, that why is it, does it not go through the courts?

Why cannot people and companies overseas who have breached New Zealand regulations, which are not set in stone, it could change at any time with any government. Why can they not be heard before a magistrate or a jury? Why can they not actually appeal their case?

Why is it that this one person has so much power in our country? It's not good. And I just think that social media is a great leveler. I think that alternative media is a great leveler and and we need that infrastructure because we don't have enough of it in New Zealand.

'cause we're only 5 million people and we need to be able to put ourselves out there to other countries. And you can't rely on the mainstream media to do that. They're underfunded. They're under-resourced. There's only so much that they can do. There's only so many stories that they can cover. We actually need as many different media outlets as possible, even the amateur ones.

Um, I'm Sue Gray, the leader of the New Zealand Outdoors and Freedom Party. I specialize in my day job as a human rights and complex emerging issues lawyer.

Um, and particularly concerned about democracy and protecting democracy. With me is Dr. Allison Pavlov chooses an academic in business and law, and also our candidate for Wellington Central for the upcoming election. The New Zealand Outdoors and Freedom Party is a voice for people, communities, small business and nature.

We are a voice for fundamental rights and freedoms, ethics and local decision making. We're a voice against the globalist agenda. We are a voice for people, power and respect for fundamental rights and freedoms, respect for the New Zealand Bill of Rights and for our other laws such as privacy laws.

And we are making a submission today. We largely share the concerns of the free speech union and actually the previous submitter. Our concerns are this bill is further erosion of our fundamental rights, including freedom of expression and our rights, our protection against search and surveillance.

And particularly because not all of the decisions for search and surveillance would even go before the courts. The GCSB and the SIS already can do search and surveillance through their own protocols without judicial independent overview. So it's a slippery slope. It's a cumulative effect.

And controlling overseas media is contrary to the freedom of expression. What we need to do is look at the hierarchy of laws, fundamental rights and freedoms. Part of our constitution should be at the top, and we should not be making ad hoc law changes that come undermine those fundamental rights and freedoms.

It's very convenient to make ad hoc law changes, but it's not constitutional, it's not good government, and it's not for democracy. So we focus on our written submission on the privacy issues, and we, we stand by what we've put in the written submission. What we want to talk about more today is our bill of rights issues and how this bill undermines those rights.

We need to look not only at preserving the rights we've got, but reclaiming our rights. So if anything, we would be promoting less interference in free speech than what we've already got, rather than extending the telecommunications laws further to capture overseas organizations.

The free speech union raised a couple of very good points. One was about whistleblowers. Now I've been acting for Barry Young, who is a whistleblower who whistle blew data on the, um, health New Zealand webpage or not webpage, health New Zealand data. He has been treated abysmally over 750 days of being under police bailing conditions while his criminal processes proceed despite Parliament's law, the Protected Disclosures Act, which was supposed to protect whistleblowers, the current protections are not working.

So again, we need more protection for whistleblowers if we want to hold corrupt practices to account. The second point is, we can't say anymore that there's no corruption in New Zealand. We've had the shocking example of j Mc skimming the Deputy Police Commissioner and the police conduct resulting in the downfall of the former police commissioner Andrew Costa.

And the, the shocking findings of the IPCA. We have to be much more alert to transparency and to exposing the problems that we have in the system. We have to protect people coming forward. We have to give people the ability to communicate between themselves without state overview.

It's incredibly important in a democratic society. We've had a discussion with the previous submitter about the risk of what if our state turns corrupt. However, just looking back over the last few years, we've had so many abuses of our fundamental rights and freedoms already.

And we've had this, again, it's been described a single source of truth from the government. But the current Prime Minister was going down that path yesterday talking about there is only a, um, disinformation, all these things. Information that the government doesn't like is not disinformation. It is a different view from the government view.

And what's really important is that we can still talk about different views respectfully, and often people are misinformed. That's fine if we talk about it. We can work through these issues if we try to censor different views. We have a real problem in terms of our fundamental freedom rights, our Constitution, and the democracy of New Zealand.

So we stand at the New Zealand S and Freedom Party for the people of New Zealand, not the globalists, not other, other international agendas. We stand for the people having their fundamental rights for principled law reform that is always looking in the context of freedom freedom's first and whether changes are beneficial to the future of the people, to our children, our grandchildren, and future generations.

Let's not have these ad hoc secret changes sneak through over the Christmas holiday break, hoping that nobody will even notice 'em. Let's be transparent. Let's be a government that we can all be proud of with laws that we can all be proud of so that New Zealand can be an example of the best practice for the world.

Not some hidden agendas going on that people don't understand. If we've still got time, Alison's got a couple more points to make too.
